summary: If a benchmark task is Clasp-backed, scenario tests usually need explicit bootstrap-recovery flags on both `prepare` and `verify`; missing that looks like a coverage gap at first but fails immediately once you add the Clasp path.
ergonomics: The benchmark harness is consistent once you follow the existing Clasp-vs-TypeScript split used by nearby scripts like `test-audit-log.sh`.
The bootstrap-recovery guard in `run-benchmark.mjs` is helpful, but it means copying a TypeScript-only scenario test to Clasp without mirroring those flags will break.
follow_ups: Consider factoring the repeated Clasp/TypeScript scenario-test pattern into a small shared shell helper to avoid future one-sided benchmark coverage.
Consider adding a benchmark test that also asserts `mixed-stack-semantic-layer` command logs include both interop-boundary task ids explicitly, since that suite now depends on this pair.
warnings: A benchmark scenario script that only prepares/verifies the TypeScript side can still let `bash scripts/verify-all.sh` pass even when a new mirrored Clasp benchmark member lacks end-to-end coverage.
For Clasp benchmark tasks, `run-benchmark.mjs prepare|verify` will reject the default path unless `--allow-bootstrap-recovery true` is passed.
residual_risks: `benchmarks/test-interop-boundary.sh` still checks pass/fail behavior via the benchmark verifier rather than asserting the emitted JSON payload inline, so regressions in exact blame text remain covered indirectly through the task-level verify script.
